item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Image originates from here, which is under All Rights Reserved. AS such, it is not compliant with CC 3.0, and has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmmm, I like this one a lot. It gave me a really powerful sense of poignancy, of nostalgia, of things lost. I like it more than the photo album (even though, after reading that, I felt like I knew what this was going to do), and I'm looking forward to reading the final item in the series.

Also, I would definitely spend hours at a time sitting on this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nope. I left it at "unknown" because I didn't want the text to overlap into three lines.

This was probably not a good reason. I don't mind the apparent edit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is the picture caption incomplete? It seems like there was supposed to be a couple more words at the end there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry, I don't usually check my comments. Just realized how to in fact.
Anyway, the "Wispy" was referring to comments like "As if in response to a…" actually, all of the "as if" phrases. It is speculation in my eyes.

Plus, you really use the phrase "As if" often.

As for the bothersome errors, that was just what I talked about in my previous post. That's all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Taste is taste, but I'll actually take that as a compliment: "wispy", or perhaps "wistful" is exactly the tone I was trying to create. Mood can get lost in scientific presentation, but I really don't think one needs to be sacrificed for the other. This is also a skip that effects the emotions, not just the senses.

Can you point out any specific instance of "unscientific" language?

"Seating" was just a misspelling— thanks for catching it. Was that what you meant by "bothersome errors" or was there something else?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't like the tone. It felt too… Wispy, if that makes any sense.
It is how a writer would write an article, but not how a scientist would write a report. (Unless the scientist was an amateur writer, in which case he/she would probably get yelled at by his/her boss and someone else would write the report.)

Also

Seating? Seat isn't a verb and doesn't have a verb form (Rather it is a noun) as far as I know. That was a jarring error that bothered me for the rest of the piece.

No vote because the article doesn't infuriate me or give me a headache, but has bothersome errors and an off tone.

(PS, the writing is good. The imagery you used, very nice and all. I just don't think this is how a scientific report on an anomalous object would look)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very nice - a nice addition to the Bonifay canon you seem to be building. I was unsure whether you planned to build on the hinted tragedy in future SCPs or whether this was intended to stand alone, but it works well either way.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad you pointed that out, because I initially came up with 1) a story based on a huge online archive of Florida historical photos 2) three items from that story that retained anomalous properties 3) how I'd write them as SCPs. Posting them one-by-one was risky because I actually think they work best as fragments telling a single narrative, but I also didn't want to write up one huge thing and have it flop— I thought SCP-1890 was the most oatmeal of the ideas, and since it stuck I feel more confident about weaving the pieces together.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for noting that, I was a little apprehensive about the idea of interconnected "heirloom" SCPs, but it was definitely how I wanted to write them; I probably could have posted them all together, but I wanted each to stand on their own and have their own testing process/containment procedures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The combination of subtle tragedy and mystery in the final test log was very well done. I predict that the backstory to this series of family heirlooms is going to be really depressing…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Beautiful and haunting. I'm with Scantron, too. I didn't find 1890 all that interesting, but as the inception of a collection of items, it's a good concept and I'm interested to see what else comes of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Y'know, I like this, but somehow the 1890 crosslink makes it work for me more, despite me not liking 1890 much. Probably because that article's execution isn't so much as stake here as the general concept of the item itself, and knowing that 1896 ties in with an item like that helps.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I did (over) think about that, and I kept it for two headcanon reasons— 1) this is stored at a safe item research facility where the priorities are less "contain it so it can't kill again" and more "contain and study the extent of this item's paranormal qualities", so 2) they wouldn't have just started hacking it with an axe, it would have been some minor abrasions— leading to the worthwhile discovery that in "suspend state" the rope becomes virtually indestructible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Attempts to sever the rope while the object is suspended have been unsuccessful.

Why would they even try to do that in the first place? Before the first attempt, for all they knew, it could have rendered the SCP non-anomalous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This thing is, I'm not sure that the Foundation is going to go into detail about aspects of the object that aren't directly related to its function. It's a tire. Call it a tire.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hm… I initially wasn't sure if I was going to include the picture, and there's a distinct difference in the look/feel of old tires and modern tires. It's also the only direct reference to the swing's age— it isn't something they nicked of some kid's porch. I might rephrase it, but I think the detail is important.

And the dates in SCP-1890 are based off of an actual collection of Florida historical photographs, with a date range from about 1896 to 1930; I am pretty sure I specified "early 18th century" in the article, but I'll make sure. It isn't meant to be exact: these are generational items.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, I'm glad you mentioned that— that was probably the part I spent the most time trying to get to sound right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, it's a bit rough to say she can't even reference her own SCPs. She's in the best position to do so well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Please do not give people bad advice. Cross-referencing is fine when it adds to the SCP doing it. Also, the author seems to be building a small mythology around a group of her own SCPs, and I like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A similar tire swing has been identified in ten of the twenty-six photographs contained in SCP-1890.

You know, Cross-referencing SCPs is frowned upon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. Also, I think this date and the SCP-1890 dates aren't consistent?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting…
I wonder if there are any objects that haven't been made into SCPs yet…
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this series. It's quirky, and interesting, without being over the top. Please, continue writing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
resembling those manufactured in the 1920s.

I'd prefer it if you left this phrase out. It's anecdotal and doesn't fit with tone. Otherwise, +1 for not having it murder people in stupid ways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, this. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nicely done. Weird without being scary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not know if like article or like scene article sets. Like, though.

-edit-

Know now. Like article. Tone dry, clinical, but not stilted. That not easy to pull off.

Visual and emotional impression of test subjects reactions to object conveyed effectively, especially in interview. Felt like interviewee was real person recalling dreamlike experience that not fully understand. That also not easy to pull off.

Would give cookie, but ate all cookie. Give +1 instead.
